result: Rain has always been my quiet teacher, appearing without announcement, swallowing the dust off the streets and the heat out of crowded rooms. When I was young, I would sit pressed against the window, tracing the crooked rivulets that tried to outrun one another. Each drop seemed to carry gossip from faraway places, secrets tapped in Morse code across the glass. Now I walk under the rain instead, feeling its chill dig playful fingertips along the collar of my coat. I listen for the rhythms nested within the downpour: the big drum of gutter pools, the whisper symbols of falling leaves, the syncopated applause from passing tires. A neighborhood under rain becomes a different country. Neon signs blur into watercolor patches, and the bakery’s cinnamon warmth feels like an embassy offering visas to anyone with wet shoes. Strangers exchange these brief, wry smiles that say, Yes, we are both soaked, and yes, we will both survive. Rain also edits the noise inside my head. Lists, ambitions, grudges, anxieties—they all shrink beneath the percussion. I remember that being a person is mostly about absorbing weather, letting seasons move through our habits. Recently, I walked home without an umbrella, letting the storm map itself onto my clothes. Halfway there, I noticed sprouts pushing through the cracks in the sidewalk, radiant, defiant. They made me laugh. Here were tiny green declarations insisting the world is still possible. I whispered a thank-you to the rain, and hurried on, dripping yet lighter. Into the night.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
